screens.heading: One issuer, every maturity
screens.body: Line up one borrower's bonds — say, the US Treasury's — from shortest maturity to longest, and plot each one's yield. That line is the yield curve: the market's price of time at every horizon, in one picture. Most of the time it slopes upward — lenders want more to lock money away for longer.

screens.heading: One curve, three hats
screens.body: The same curve does three jobs. It is the ANCHOR: the risk-free base other assets are priced off. It is a SIGNAL: its shape carries information about the market's macro view. And it is a TOOLKIT: from its prices you can extract discount factors, spot rates, and forward rates that price any fixed cash flow. Always know which hat the curve is wearing in a given conversation.

screens.heading: The toolkit, briefly
screens.body: A spot rate is the pure yield for a single payment at one future date. A forward rate is the rate the curve implies for a period beginning in the future — today's 1-year and 2-year yields together imply a rate for the year starting one year from now. Extracting spots and forwards from bond prices turns the curve into a pricing machine.

screens.heading: "Priced in" is not "predicted"
screens.body: When a strategist says the market 'has cuts priced in,' they are reading forward rates. Forwards are break-evens, not forecasts: they are the future rates at which rolling short bonds and holding long bonds would pay the same. They also embed a term premium — extra compensation demanded for holding longer bonds — so the forward path and the market's genuine expected path can differ. What the curve pays you and what it predicts are two different questions.

screens.heading: When the curve inverts
screens.body: Occasionally short yields sit ABOVE long yields — an inverted curve. The primary beginner reading, and the historically useful one, is expectations: the market anticipates that today's high short rates will fall, which tends to accompany expectations of slowdown and central-bank cuts. But shape has more than one driver — a shrinking or negative term premium, and supply-and-demand pressures from investors with strong maturity preferences, can bend the curve too. Expectations first; one mechanism is not the whole story.
